text.fullText: Perez, JuanB From: Susan Eastwood <sce4321@gmail.com> Sent: Monday, February 16, 2026 12:47 PM To: OW-Docket Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 You don't often get email from sce4321@gmail.com. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Comment on Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Thank you for the opportunity to provide comments in strong opposition the updates to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act proposed in Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations. My name is Susan Eastwood, and I am a resident of Ashford, Connecticut. I am Chair of the Ashford Clean Energy Task Force, a member of the Connecticut Clean Energy Network (CTEN) Steering Committee, and a Volunteer Leader at Sierra Club Connecticut. In my professional career, I worked to protect clean water and public health, including work on clarifying the definitions in the Clean Water Act to be sure that they covered wetlands, streams, and source waters that would surely flow into larger navigable waterways, bringing toxic pollution and microplastics along. I urge the EPA to reconsider its recent update of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act limiting the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and possibly block them if they threaten waterways, wetlands, and ultimately, clean, drinking water. I am very concerned that these proposed updates, if adopted, will result in great harm to our public health and environmental quality. The Clean Water Act is the nation’s most effective tool to protect the waterways in all our communities. Section 401 has been critical to putting the public's interest above the wants of polluters. After recent rollbacks of federal clean water protections, state, tribal and local input has become even more necessary. Defending state and tribal authority under the Clean Water Act is essential to providing states and tribes a say in protecting water quality, promoting public health, and ensuring environmental justice for all. The Trump administration’s rollback of state and tribal authority under the Clean Water Act is intended to fast track projects and save developers money while making it harder for states and tribes to limit water quality harms from these projects. This puts water quality at risk for millions of Americans. I my decades of work to protect our environment and public health, I have learned that clean water is the highest environmental priority for most Americans. The EPA's proposed rule weakening Section 401 would contradict the public will, as well as the public good. Americans want clean water protections and 1 the ability of states and tribes to review and possibly block projects that may threaten public health and degrade the waterways and wetlands within their borders. I urge the EPA to reject the proposed revisions to Section 401. Thank you for your consideration of my comments. Sincerely, Susan Eastwood Ashford, CT ____________ "What you do makes a difference, and you have to decide what kind of a difference you want to make." - Dr. Jane Goodall 2
